mnemonics.title: MESI Protocol States
mnemonics.content: ME-SI: Modified (M) → Exclusive dirty data | Exclusive (E) → Exclusive clean | Shared (S) → Multiple copies clean | Invalid (I) → Not present/stale. Think: 'My Eat Sandwich Immediately'—or recall MESI as 'Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid'.

mnemonics.title: False Sharing Detection
mnemonics.content: F.A.L.S.E. → Frequent writes, Adjacent data, Low spatial locality illusion, Sharing cache line, Eviction/reload overhead. Remember: FALSE = Frequently Accessed Locations Sharing cache lines.

mnemonics.title: Padding Formula
mnemonics.content: For N-byte cache line and variable of size S: pad = ((N - (S % N)) % N). Example: 64-byte line, 8-byte long: pad = 64 - (8 % 64) = 56 bytes. Use: struct { long counter; char pad[56]; }.

acronyms.usage: Four-state cache coherence protocol used in write-invalidate systems. A line is Modified (dirty, exclusive), Exclusive (clean, exclusive), Shared (clean, possibly multiple), or Invalid (not present).

acronyms.expansionExtended: Adds 'Owned' state for systems with write-back caches where one cache supplies data to others while retaining it.
Simplified three-state protocol; Modified (dirty), Shared (clean, multiple), Invalid (not present). Predecessor to MESI.

acronyms.usage: Memory latency varies based on distance from CPU. False sharing effects are more severe in NUMA systems due to cross-node memory access costs.
Multiple hardware threads share execution resources. False sharing between SMT threads can be particularly harmful due to shared cache hierarchy.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a memory block as a 64-byte row. Show two adjacent variables (8 bytes each) occupying bytes 0-7 and 8-15. When Thread A writes to var1 and Thread B writes to var2, draw invalidation arrows bouncing between their caches—showing how independent writes cause cache line ping-pong.

Create a state diagram: Modified ↔ Exclusive ↔ Shared ↔ Invalid. Label transitions: Read hit (stay in state), Write hit (M/E/S → M), Read miss on bus (M → S, supply data), Invalidate on bus (any → I). Show bus transaction arrows between caches.

Show array elements as a grid. Color-code cache lines (e.g., 8 elements per 64-byte line). When threads update alternating elements, draw 'hot' zones where invalidations occur. Compare to padded version where each element gets its own line.

analogies.analogy: Think of two people editing different pages of the same physical notebook. Every time one person writes, the other person's copy of the entire notebook is taken away and replaced with a fresh one—even though they're working on completely different pages! The 'notebook' is the cache line, and the 'pages' are individual variables.

Like a shared Google document where everyone sees real-time updates. If Person A types on a shared document, Person B immediately sees it. In computers, caches must 'see' each other's writes through coherence protocols—the hardware equivalent of constantly refreshing the document view.

Imagine a library book that was recalled. Your copy is now 'Invalid'—you can't read it anymore. To use it, you must go back to the library (main memory) and check out a fresh copy. This is what happens when a cache line is invalidated by another core's write.
